notes: All assertions pass in both configurations (100% pass rate) — current assertions check for output presence and keyword coverage, which both configs handle well. More discriminating assertions should target skill-specific behaviours: structured multi-agent phases, execution trace depth, acceptance criteria granularity.

with_skill uses +17317 tokens and +91.4s on average — the cost of 3-agent orchestration. For complex Jira tickets with deep codebases this overhead buys structured design docs and traceable phase execution.
without_skill produces correct, working implementations but skips the design phase — no codebase exploration plan, no explicit acceptance criteria mapping, no test scenario planning.
Eval 2 (rate-limiting) was the heaviest eval for both configs: with_skill 414.7s/62716 tokens, without_skill 335.1s/48739 tokens. The extra Redis + Flask scaffold complexity explains the gap.

The skill correctly routes all 3 evals through the direct-requirements path (Phase 1 Jira fetch skipped), matching the casual/explicit requirement inputs. A Jira-ticket eval would test Phase 1 fetch and MCP tool integration.
